title: US House votes to reopen Department of Homeland Security after record 76-day shutdown
summary: The US House of Representatives voted to reopen the majority of the Department of Homeland Security, ending the longest agency shutdown in American history at 76 days, with implications for border security, immigration enforcement, and FEMA disaster response capabilities.
contents:
## DHS Reopens After Longest Agency Shutdown in US History

### Congressional Action
The US House of Representatives voted to reopen the majority of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), bringing an end to the longest single-agency federal government shutdown in American history. The shutdown lasted 76 days.

### Impact Summary
The extended DHS closure affected multiple critical government functions:
- Border security operations operated at reduced capacity
- Immigration enforcement actions faced significant delays
- FEMA disaster response capabilities experienced degradation
- TSA airport security staffing strained throughout the period
- Customs and Border Protection processing slowed at ports of entry

### Resolution Details
The reopening legislation passed with bipartisan support, reflecting mounting pressure from both parties as the shutdown's effects rippled through communities dependent on DHS services. The vote restores funding for the majority of DHS operations.

### Historical Significance
The 76-day shutdown surpassed the previous record for a single-agency closure, generating intense public debate about the use of government shutdowns as political leverage. The resolution represents a significant political development in ongoing budget and policy negotiations.
